tweets.text:
Part of me thinks Saylor announcing Strategy’s Bitcoin sale kicks the can down the road *slightly*… aka maybe we don’t see a capitulation event in the market this week -

But still believe 30s/40s for BTC are imminent.

When there’s blood in the water, it’s only a matter of time before the market tries to hunt it down for more.

thread_tweets.text:

thread_tweets.full_text:
Part of me thinks Saylor announcing Strategy’s Bitcoin sale kicks the can down the road *slightly*… aka maybe we don’t see a capitulation event in the market this week -

But still believe 30s/40s for BTC are imminent.

When there’s blood in the water, it’s only a matter of time before the market tries to hunt it down for more.

thread_tweets.text:
And for what it’s worth - I believe BTC finds its bottom around the same time Strategy has enough cash set aside to at least wipe away the $6.7B of convertible debt sitting on its books.

That doesn’t necessarily mean MSTR bottoms at the same time.

It’s important to remember Bitcoin isn’t defined by one man or one company.

tweets.text: Trump rang the opening bell today to ring in his new Trump accounts. But there is nothing to celebrate, as the $1,000 federal contribution to these accounts is funded by issuing more debt. So the kids who get the money also inherit the debt. Cutting spending would be far better.

tweets.text: $MSTR now has a completely different business model. Instead of selling common and preferred stock and issuing debt to buy Bitcoin, the new strategy is to sell Bitcoin to pay interest and dividends, pay off debt, buy back shares it sold, and hope that Bitcoin’s price goes way up.

thread_tweets.text: $MSTR now has a completely different business model. Instead of selling common and preferred stock and issuing debt to buy Bitcoin, the new strategy is to sell Bitcoin to pay interest and dividends, pay off debt, buy back shares it sold, and hope that Bitcoin’s price goes way up.
thread_tweets.full_text: $MSTR now has a completely different business model. Instead of selling common and preferred stock and issuing debt to buy Bitcoin, the new strategy is to sell Bitcoin to pay interest and dividends, pay off debt, buy back shares it sold, and hope that Bitcoin’s price goes way up.

thread_tweets.text: @nycguy1988 Because the common stock trades at a large discount to the Bitcoin per share. So selling Bitcoin is much better for common stock owners.
thread_tweets.full_text: @nycguy1988 Because the common stock trades at a large discount to the Bitcoin per share. So selling Bitcoin is much better for common stock owners.

tweets.text: $MSTR spent the last two weeks selling Bitcoin. The average price on 3,588 Bitcoin sold was $60,196.73. Given MSTR’s average cost, that’s a realized loss of about $15K per Bitcoin, or about $54 million. With over 840K Bitcoin left to sell, the total losses will be much greater.

thread_tweets.text: $MSTR spent the last two weeks selling Bitcoin. The average price on 3,588 Bitcoin sold was $60,196.73. Given MSTR’s average cost, that’s a realized loss of about $15K per Bitcoin, or about $54 million. With over 840K Bitcoin left to sell, the total losses will be much greater.

thread_tweets.text: @Cheeffjeef @Jeremybtc It's larger when you subtract supply that doesn't really count. But the key is not the perecentage MSTR owns. It's what happens to the market when the biggest buyer becomes the biggest seller.
thread_tweets.full_text: @Cheeffjeef @Jeremybtc It's larger when you subtract supply that doesn't really count. But the key is not the perecentage MSTR owns. It's what happens to the market when the biggest buyer becomes the biggest seller.

tweets.text: @saylor So over a two-week period, the average price you received on your Bitcoin sales was $60,196.73. How do you manage to get such bad execution? When you buy, you buy the highs; when you sell, you sell the lows. Whoever is handling your order flow is likely making a killing.
